section.title: The Remaining Tribes
section.sectionRationale: The distribution of the land is completed. By lot at Shiloh, Simeon, Zebulun, Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan each receive their inheritance, and at the very end, only after every tribe is settled, Joshua himself receives his portion, the town he requested. It is one movement, the finishing of the allotment, which shows God faithful to give each tribe its place and Joshua modeling the servant-leader who takes his own inheritance last.
section.thesis: God completes the inheritance for every tribe by lot at the sanctuary, keeping His promise down to the last portion, and the leader who had conquered the land takes nothing for himself until all the people are provided for, a picture of servant leadership that points to the greater Servant who secures His people's inheritance before His own glory.
section.frameNote: Seven beats: Simeon's inheritance within Judah, verses 1 to 9; Zebulun, verses 10 to 16; Issachar, verses 17 to 23; Asher, verses 24 to 31; Naphtali, verses 32 to 39; Dan, verses 40 to 48; and Joshua's own inheritance last of all, verses 49 to 51.

units.label: Simeon's inheritance within Judah
units.note: their inheritance lay within the territory of Judah

verses.text: The second {{goral|lot}} came out for the clans of the tribe of Simeon: Their inheritance lay within the territory of Judah
and included Beersheba (or Sheba), Moladah,
Hazar-shual, Balah, Ezem,
Eltolad, Bethul, Hormah,
Ziklag, Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susah,
Beth-lebaoth, and Sharuhen, thirteen cities, along with their villages.
Ain, Rimmon, Ether, and Ashan, four cities, along with their villages,
and all the villages surrounding these cities as far as Baalath-beer (Ramah of the Negev). This was the inheritance of the clans of the tribe of Simeon.

The inheritance of the Simeonites was taken from the territory of Judah, because the share for Judah’s descendants was too large for them. So the Simeonites received an inheritance within Judah’s portion.

units.label: Zebulun's inheritance
units.note: the third lot came up for Zebulun

verses.text: The third lot came up for the clans of the tribe of Zebulun: The border of their inheritance stretched as far as Sarid.
It went up westward to Maralah, reached Dabbesheth, and met the brook east of Jokneam.
From Sarid it turned eastward along the border of Chisloth-tabor and went on to Daberath and up to Japhia.
From there it crossed eastward to Gath-hepher and to Eth-kazin; it extended to Rimmon and curved around toward Neah.
Then the border circled around the north side of Neah to Hannathon and ended at the Valley of Iphtah-el.
It also included Kattath, Nahalal, Shimron, Idalah, and Bethlehem. There were twelve cities, along with their villages.
This was the inheritance of the clans of the tribe of Zebulun, including these cities and their villages.

units.label: Issachar's inheritance
units.note: the fourth lot came out for Issachar

verses.text: The fourth lot came out for the clans of the tribe of Issachar:
Their territory included Jezreel, Chesulloth, Shunem,
Hapharaim, Shion, Anaharath,
Rabbith, Kishion, Ebez,
Remeth, En-gannim, En-haddah, and Beth-pazzez.
The border reached Tabor, Shahazumah, and Beth-shemesh, and ended at the Jordan. There were sixteen cities, along with their villages.
This was the inheritance of the clans of the tribe of Issachar, including these cities and their villages.

units.label: Asher's inheritance
units.note: the fifth lot came out for Asher

verses.text: The fifth lot came out for the clans of the tribe of Asher:
Their territory included Helkath, Hali, Beten, Achshaph,
Allammelech, Amad, and Mishal. On the west the border touched Carmel and Shihor-libnath,
then turned eastward toward Beth-dagon, touched Zebulun and the Valley of Iphtah-el, and went north to Beth-emek and Neiel, passing Cabul on the left.
It went on to Ebron, Rehob, Hammon, and Kanah, as far as Greater Sidon.
The border then turned back toward Ramah as far as the fortified city of Tyre, turned toward Hosah, and came out at the Sea in the region of Achzib,
Ummah, Aphek, and Rehob. There were twenty-two cities, along with their villages.
This was the inheritance of the clans of the tribe of Asher, including these cities and their villages.

units.label: Naphtali's inheritance
units.note: the sixth lot came out for Naphtali

verses.text: The sixth lot came out for the clans of the tribe of Naphtali:
Their border started at Heleph and the great tree of Zaanannim, passing Adami-nekeb and Jabneel as far as Lakkum and ending at the Jordan.
Then the border turned westward to Aznoth-tabor and ran from there to Hukkok, touching Zebulun on the south side, Asher on the west, and Judah at the Jordan on the east.
The fortified cities were Ziddim, Zer, Hammath, Rakkath, Chinnereth,
Adamah, Ramah, Hazor,
Kedesh, Edrei, En-hazor,
Iron, Migdal-el, Horem, Beth-anath, and Beth-shemesh. There were nineteen cities, along with their villages.
This was the inheritance of the clans of the tribe of Naphtali, including these cities and their villages.

units.label: Dan's inheritance and Joshua's portion last of all
units.note: they gave Joshua an inheritance among them

verses.text: The seventh lot came out for the clans of the tribe of Dan:
The territory of their inheritance included Zorah, Eshtaol, Ir-shemesh,
Shaalabbin, Aijalon, Ithlah,
Elon, Timnah, Ekron,
Eltekeh, Gibbethon, Baalath,
Jehud, Bene-berak, Gath-rimmon,
Me-jarkon, and Rakkon, including the territory across from Joppa.

(Later, when the territory of the Danites was lost to them, they went up and fought against Leshem, captured it, and put it to the sword. So they took {{yarash|possession}} of Leshem, settled there, and renamed it after their father Dan.)
This was the inheritance of the clans of the tribe of Dan, including these cities and their villages.
When they had finished distributing the land into its territories, the Israelites gave Joshua son of Nun an {{nachalah|inheritance}} among them,
as the LORD had commanded. They gave him the city of Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim, as he requested. He rebuilt the city and settled in it.

These are the inheritances that Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the families distributed by lot to the tribes of Israel at Shiloh before the LORD at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting. So they finished dividing up the land.

goral.gloss: A lot, cast to determine God's decision. Each remaining tribe receives its portion by goral, so the whole distribution is seen as the LORD's own doing, not human negotiation. The repeated the lot came out for underlines that every tribe's place in the land was assigned by God, who orders the boundaries of His people's dwelling.

yarash.gloss: To take possession of, dispossess. When Dan lost its allotted coastal territory to enemy pressure, they went north and yarash, took possession of, Leshem, renaming it Dan. The note quietly signals both the difficulty Dan faced in its original portion and the ongoing struggle to possess the land, part of the incomplete conquest running through these chapters.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession. After every tribe has received its nachalah, the people give Joshua his, the town he requested. That the conquering leader takes his inheritance last, only when all are provided for, models a servant leadership that seeks the people's good before its own, a pattern fulfilled in Christ.

basic.context: This chapter finishes the division of the land. One by one, the remaining tribes receive their inheritance by lot at Shiloh: Simeon (whose land lies inside Judah's large territory), Zebulun, Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and Dan. There is a poignant note about Dan, whose coastal land was hard to hold, so they later migrated north and took a new city. Then comes the quiet climax: only after every tribe is settled do the people give Joshua his own inheritance, the modest town of Timnath-serah that he had requested. The great leader who had led the conquest waited until last and took a small portion. The chapter ends, so they finished dividing up the land. The question underneath is: what does it look like when God faithfully provides for all His people, and when a leader puts the people before himself?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative completing the allotment section, mostly land records with two telling notes: Simeon absorbed into Judah, and Joshua's inheritance taken last. The geography testifies to God's faithfulness in settling every tribe. Joshua's self-effacing example is presented positively and points to servant leadership. Dan's difficulty continues the honest thread of incomplete conquest.
basic.originalLanguages: Each tribe receives its portion by goral, by lot, as God's assignment. Dan, pressed out of its land, went and yarash, took possession of, a northern city. And Joshua receives his nachalah, his inheritance, last of all, after every tribe is provided for.
basic.typology: Joshua taking his inheritance last, after securing everyone else's, foreshadows Christ, the leader who seeks His people's good before His own glory, securing their inheritance through His self-giving. God's faithfulness in settling every tribe pictures the certainty of the inheritance He gives all His people.
basic.christ: Joshua, the conquering leader, waits to receive his own portion until all the people are settled, a faint shadow of the greater Joshua, Jesus, who did not grasp His glory but emptied Himself to secure His people's inheritance, and is exalted only after accomplishing their salvation. The completed allotment points to the inheritance Christ guarantees to all who are His.
basic.god: God is faithful to provide for every tribe, down to the last, ordering each portion by lot. He is the God who cares for the whole people, not just the prominent tribes, giving even absorbed Simeon and struggling Dan a place. And He honors the servant who puts others first, as the people give Joshua his inheritance at the end.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 49 foretold Simeon being scattered in Israel, fulfilled in his absorption into Judah. Philippians 2 shows Christ emptying Himself and being exalted. Mark 10 says the greatest must be servant of all. And Joshua 21 concludes that not one of God's promises failed.
basic.oneStory: God completes the inheritance for every tribe, keeping His promise to the last portion, and Joshua the conquering leader takes his own share last of all. The faithful provision for the whole people and the servant leader who waits until the end point forward to Christ, who secures His people's inheritance before His own glory and settles them forever in the land of promise.

context.literary: Chapter 19 completes the tribal allotments begun in chapter 15, running through the remaining tribes in order and closing with a summary, so they finished dividing up the land, verse 51. The pattern the lot came out for structures each tribe's section. Two departures from the pattern carry meaning: Simeon's inheritance is explained as drawn from Judah's oversized portion, and the chapter, and the whole allotment, ends not with a tribe but with Joshua's own inheritance, deliberately placed last. Dan's note about migration, verse 47, looks ahead to events in Judges 18.
context.historical: Simeon's territory lay within Judah's, and the tribe was gradually absorbed, fulfilling Jacob's word that Simeon would be scattered in Israel, Genesis 49:7. Zebulun, Issachar, Asher, and Naphtali settled the fertile north around Galilee and its hills; Naphtali and Zebulun's territory would later be called Galilee of the nations, the region of Jesus' ministry, Isaiah 9:1 and Matthew 4:15-16. Dan's original coastal allotment near the Philistines proved hard to hold, so part of the tribe migrated north to Leshem, renamed Dan. Joshua's town, Timnath-serah, in Ephraim's hill country, was a modest portion for the nation's leader, taken only after all others were settled.
context.canonical: The completed allotment fulfills God's promise to give each tribe its inheritance, and Joshua will soon declare that not one of all God's good promises failed, 21:45. Simeon's absorption into Judah fulfills Jacob's prophecy. The northern tribal territories, especially Zebulun and Naphtali, become the setting for the dawning of Messiah's light, Isaiah 9:1-2. Joshua's example of taking his inheritance last anticipates the New Testament ideal of servant leadership, supremely embodied in Christ, who came not to be served but to serve and give His life as a ransom, Mark 10:43-45.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the completion of the tribal allotments by lot at Shiloh, showing God faithful to settle every tribe in its inheritance, and to highlight, by placing it last, Joshua's example of receiving his own portion only after all the people were provided for.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The land records describe Israel's historical settlement. What is held up for imitation is Joshua's servant leadership in taking his inheritance last, and what is affirmed is God's faithfulness to provide for the whole people. Dan's struggle and migration are recorded honestly, not endorsed, continuing the theme of incomplete possession.

debates.question: What is the significance of Joshua receiving his inheritance last?

views.label: A model of servant leadership
views.case: The leader who led the conquest and oversaw the distribution does not seize the best land or take his portion first; he waits until every tribe is settled and then receives a modest town he requested, embodying the leader who seeks the people's good before his own, a pattern later crystallized in Jesus' teaching that the greatest must be servant of all, Mark 10:43-45.

views.label: A testimony to God's order and Joshua's contentment
views.case: That Joshua receives his inheritance as the LORD had commanded and asks only for one town shows both submission to God's order and personal contentment; he does not use his authority for self-enrichment but trusts God for his portion, closing the allotment on a note of humble faith rather than self-interest.

debates.bearing: Both readings commend Joshua: his placing himself last displays servant leadership and contentment, pointing to the greater Servant who secured His people's inheritance before entering His own glory.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words tie the completion of the allotment to God's hand and to servant leadership. Each remaining tribe receives its portion by goral, by lot; the refrain the lot came out for repeats through the chapter, stamping every boundary as the LORD's own assignment, for He orders the times and places where His people dwell, Acts 17:26. Along the way, one honest note: when Dan could not hold its coastal land, they went north and yarash, took possession of, the city of Leshem, a reminder that possessing the inheritance remained a struggle. Then comes the quiet climax built on nachalah, inheritance: when they had finished distributing the land, the people gave Joshua his nachalah, the town he had asked for, and he receives it last of all, only after every tribe is settled. The conquering leader takes nothing for himself until the whole people are provided for. Lot, possession, and an inheritance received last: the vocabulary shows God faithfully settling His people and a leader who serves them before himself, both pointing to Christ.

typology.type: Joshua taking his inheritance last, after all the people are settled
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who did not grasp His glory but served and secured His people's inheritance before entering His exaltation, Philippians 2:5-11 and Mark 10:43-45.

typology.type: God faithfully settling every tribe in its portion
typology.fulfillment: The certainty of the inheritance God gives all His people in Christ, none overlooked, 1 Peter 1:4 and John 14:2-3.

typology.type: The northern tribal lands of Zebulun and Naphtali
typology.fulfillment: Galilee where the light of Messiah dawned, Christ beginning His ministry there, Isaiah 9:1-2 and Matthew 4:15-16.

study.christ: The most striking detail in this chapter is its ending: after every tribe has received its inheritance, the leader who conquered the land takes his own portion last, and only a modest town at that. It is a faint but real shadow of the Lord Jesus. Joshua secures the people's inheritance before receiving his own; Christ, though He was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant, and humbled Himself to death on a cross to secure His people's inheritance, and only then was highly exalted, Philippians 2:5-11. Jesus taught this very shape of leadership: whoever would be great among you must be your servant, for even the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give His life as a ransom for many, Mark 10:43-45. And the northern territories of Zebulun and Naphtali, quietly listed here, would one day be the Galilee where the great light dawned, where the greater Joshua began to preach the kingdom, Matthew 4:15-16. The finished allotment, with the servant leader last, points to Christ who secures our inheritance and settles us forever.
study.god: The God of this chapter is faithful, attentive, and honoring of humility. He is faithful, completing the inheritance for every tribe so that the allotment can close with the words, so they finished dividing up the land; not one tribe is forgotten, not even Simeon absorbed into Judah or Dan struggling to hold its coast. He is attentive to the whole people, ordering each portion by lot in His own presence at the sanctuary, for He determines the boundaries of His people's dwelling. And He is a God who honors the servant, so that the leader who put the people first receives his inheritance at the end, as the LORD had commanded. The God who settles His people in the land is the same God who exalts the humble and who will, in Christ, secure and give the everlasting inheritance to all who are His.

crossReferences.note: Jacob foretold that Simeon would be scattered in Israel, fulfilled in Simeon's absorption into Judah's territory.
The greatest must be servant of all, and the Son of Man came to serve and give His life, the pattern Joshua faintly shows.
Christ humbled Himself to secure His people's good and was then exalted, the greater reality behind Joshua's taking his portion last.
Zebulun and Naphtali, Galilee of the nations, where the light of Christ's ministry dawned.
Not one word of all God's good promises failed, the faithfulness this completed allotment displays.

study.oneStory: God completes the inheritance for every tribe, keeping His promise down to the last portion, and Joshua, the conquering leader, receives his own share last of all, only when the whole people are settled. The faithful provision for all and the servant leader who waits until the end point forward to Christ, who secures His people's inheritance before entering His glory, and whose light would one day dawn in the very northern lands here divided, settling His people forever in the inheritance He guarantees.

quiz.q: Where did the tribe of Simeon receive its inheritance?

options.text: Within the territory of Judah, because Judah's portion was too large.
options.note: Verses 1 and 9 explain Simeon's land was drawn from Judah's oversized share.

options.text: In the far north near Naphtali.
options.note: Simeon's land lay within Judah in the south, not in the far north.

options.text: East of the Jordan with Reuben and Gad.
options.note: The eastern land went to Reuben, Gad, and half of Manasseh; Simeon's lay within Judah.

options.text: Simeon received no inheritance at all.
options.note: Simeon did receive an inheritance, drawn from within Judah's territory.

quiz.q: What was notable about when Joshua received his inheritance?

options.text: He received it last of all, only after every tribe had been settled.
options.note: Verses 49 to 51 show Joshua taking his portion last, a model of servant leadership.

options.text: He took the first and best portion for himself.
options.note: The opposite: he waited until last and took a modest town he requested.
He did receive an inheritance, the town of Timnath-serah, but only after all others.

options.text: He seized several cities by force.
options.note: He received one town as the LORD commanded, taken last, not seized by force.

quiz.q: How was each tribe's portion determined?

options.text: By lot at Shiloh, so each portion was received as the LORD's own assignment.
options.note: The refrain the lot came out for marks each portion as God's assignment.

options.text: By the size of each tribe's army.
options.note: Portions were assigned by lot, not by military strength.

options.text: By Joshua's personal decision for each.
options.note: The lot, not Joshua's preference, determined each portion before the LORD.

options.text: By a vote among the tribal leaders.
options.note: It was decided by lot before the LORD, not by a vote.

quiz.q: How does Joshua taking his inheritance last point to Christ?

options.text: As the leader who secured the people's inheritance before his own, he foreshadows Christ who served and secured His people's good before His exaltation.
options.note: Joshua's servant leadership points to Christ in Philippians 2 and Mark 10:45.

options.text: It shows that leaders deserve the largest reward first.
options.note: The opposite: Joshua put himself last, modeling servanthood, not privilege.

options.text: It teaches that leaders should never receive anything.
options.note: Joshua did receive an inheritance; the point is that he put the people first, not that leaders get nothing.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: Joshua's placing himself last clearly foreshadows Christ, the Servant who secures His people's inheritance.

questions: God faithfully settled every tribe, even the small and struggling ones. How does it comfort you that God overlooks none of His people, however overlooked you may feel?
Joshua, the great leader, took his inheritance last, after everyone else was cared for. Where is God calling you to put others' good before your own?
Each portion was assigned by lot as God's own arrangement. How does trusting that your circumstances are ordered by a faithful God change the way you receive your lot in life?
Dan struggled to hold the land it was given. Where are you facing a struggle to lay hold of what God has promised, and how can you keep trusting Him in it?
The servant leadership of Joshua points to Christ, who secured your inheritance before His own glory. How does Christ's self-giving move you to serve others this week?

translationNotes.note: Simeon's inheritance within Judah reflects Jacob's word in Genesis 49:7 that Simeon would be divided and scattered in Israel.
The BSB sets off thirteen cities with an em dash, and similarly four cities in verse 7; these have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.
This Bethlehem is a town in Zebulun's territory in the north, distinct from Bethlehem of Judah, the birthplace of David and of Christ.
Timnath-serah, Joshua's requested town, is also called Timnath-heres, as the BSB footnote notes; it was a modest portion for the nation's leader, taken last of all.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 19:1-51. Prose and boundary or city lists; line-break markers within verses were ignored and segments joined with single spaces. The em dashes in verses 6 and 7 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
